---
format:
    revealjs:
        theme: ../../styles/meds-slides-styles.scss
        slide-number: true
        chalkboard: true
        title-slide: false
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

plt.style.use('default')
fig_size_x = 7
fig_size_y = 4
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 10

# True function: third degree polynomial
def true_f(x):
    return 0.003*x**3 - 0.35*x**2 + 10*x - 20

# Create train set
np.random.seed(42)
n = 30
x_obs = np.random.uniform(0, 100, n)
y_obs = true_f(x_obs) + np.random.normal(0, 50, n)

# Create test set
n_test = 15
x_test = np.random.uniform(0, 100, n_test)
y_test = true_f(x_test) + np.random.normal(0, 50, n_test)

x = np.linspace(0, 100, 200)

# Linear fit
lin_model = LinearRegression()
lin_model.fit(x_obs.reshape(-1, 1), y_obs)
y_linear = lin_model.predict(x.reshape(-1, 1))

# Coefficients
beta_0 = lin_model.intercept_
beta_1 = lin_model.coef_[0]
eq_string = f"{beta_1:.2f}x + {beta_0:.2f}"

# Linear train and test MSE
y_hat_obs = lin_model.predict(x_obs.reshape(-1, 1))
train_mse = np.mean((y_obs - y_hat_obs)**2)

y_hat_test = lin_model.predict(x_test.reshape(-1, 1))
test_mse = np.mean((y_test - y_hat_test)**2)

# Degree 3 polynomial
coeffs3 = np.polyfit(x_obs, y_obs, 3)
y_poly3 = np.polyval(coeffs3, x)

# Degree 15 polynomial
t_min, t_max = x_obs.min(), x_obs.max()
x_norm = (x_obs - t_min) / (t_max - t_min)
x_line_norm = (x - t_min) / (t_max - t_min)
coeffs15 = np.polyfit(x_norm, y_obs, 15)
y_poly15 = np.polyval(coeffs15, x_line_norm)

# MSE function
def mse(y_true, y_pred):
    return np.mean((y_true - y_pred)**2)

# Predictions on train and test sets
y_hat_train_lin = lin_model.predict(x_obs.reshape(-1, 1))
y_hat_test_lin  = lin_model.predict(x_test.reshape(-1, 1))

coeffs3_pred_train = np.polyval(coeffs3, x_obs)
coeffs3_pred_test  = np.polyval(coeffs3, x_test)

x_test_norm  = (x_test - t_min) / (t_max - t_min)
x_obs_norm   = (x_obs  - t_min) / (t_max - t_min)
y_hat_train_15 = np.polyval(coeffs15, x_obs_norm)
y_hat_test_15  = np.polyval(coeffs15, x_test_norm)

train_mses = [mse(y_obs, y_hat_train_lin),
              mse(y_obs, coeffs3_pred_train),
              mse(y_obs, y_hat_train_15)]

test_mses  = [mse(y_test, y_hat_test_lin),
              mse(y_test, coeffs3_pred_test),
              mse(y_test, y_hat_test_15)]

flexibility = [1, 3, 15]
colors = ['orange', 'steelblue', 'green']

# Second training set for variance demo
np.random.seed(21)
x_obs_2 = np.random.uniform(0, 100, n)
y_obs_2 = true_f(x_obs_2) + np.random.normal(0, 50, n)

lin_model_2 = LinearRegression()
lin_model_2.fit(x_obs_2.reshape(-1, 1), y_obs_2)
y_linear_2 = lin_model_2.predict(x.reshape(-1, 1))

x_norm_2 = (x_obs_2 - t_min) / (t_max - t_min)
coeffs15_2 = np.polyfit(x_norm_2, y_obs_2, 15)
y_poly15_2 = np.polyval(coeffs15_2, x_line_norm)
```

## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"}

[EDS 232]{.custom-title}

<hr class="hr-teal">

[Lesson 3]{.custom-subtitle}

[*Training and test errors, and model flexibility*]{.custom-subtitle}

---

## {#in-this-lesson data-menu-title="In this lesson"}

[In this lesson]{.slide-title}

<hr>

- Training and test MSE

<br>

- Overfitting

<br>

- Model bias and variance

<br>

- The bias-variance tradeoff

---

## {#notation-recap data-menu-title="Notation recap"}

[Notation recap]{.slide-title}

<hr>

We have a **training set** of $n$ observed data points:

$$\{(x_1, y_1),\, \ldots,\, (x_n, y_n)\}$$


- $x_i$ = predictor information for observation $i$
- $y_i$ = response variable for observation $i$

<br>

. . .

We assume $f$ is the unknown true function, so $f(x_i) = y_i$.

We **train** a model on these data points to obtain an estimate $\hat{f}$.

<br>

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
How do we know if $\hat{f}$ is any good?
:::

---

## {#section-mse data-menu-title="# Mean Squared Error #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Mean Squared Error</p>
</div>

---

## {#mse-definition data-menu-title="Mean Squared Error"}

[Mean Squared Error (MSE)]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
To evaluate the performance of a model on a given data set, we need some way to measure how well its predictions match the observed data. 
:::


For regression, the most common way to measure how close $\hat{f}$ is to the truth is the **mean squared error (MSE)**:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \hat{f}(x_i))^2$$

<br>

. . .

- Each term $y_i - \hat{f}(x_i)$ is called a **residual** , it is the difference between the true value and the prediction
- Squaring makes all differences positive and penalizes large errors more

. . .

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
When computed on the training set, this is called the **training MSE**.
:::

---

## {#train-data-plot data-menu-title="Training data and true f"}

[Training data and true $f$]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Below we have simulated train data based on the true underlying function $f$ (black line).

```{python}
#| echo: false
plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
plt.plot(x, true_f(x), 'k-', linewidth=2, label='f (true)')
plt.scatter(x_obs, y_obs, s=30, facecolors='none', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, label='Train data')
plt.xlabel('X')
plt.ylabel('Y')
plt.xlim(0, 100)
plt.ylim(-150, 600)
plt.legend(loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#train-mse-viz data-menu-title="Visualizing the training MSE"}

[Visualizing the training MSE]{.slide-title}

<hr>

We fit a linear model to the training data and obtain the estimate:
 $\hat{f}(x)=$ `{python} eq_string`.

The **residuals** (dashed red lines) show the difference between each $y_i$ and $\hat{f}(x_i)$.

Using these we calcualte the train MSE for this $\hat{f}$ to be `{python} round(train_mse, 2)`.

```{python}
#| echo: false
plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
plt.plot(x, y_linear, color='orange', linewidth=2, label=r'$\hat{f}$ (linear fit)')
plt.scatter(x_obs, y_obs, s=30, facecolors='none', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, label='Train data')
for i, (xi, yi, yhi) in enumerate(zip(x_obs, y_obs, y_hat_train_lin)):
    plt.plot([xi, xi], [yi, yhi], 'r--', linewidth=0.8,
             label='Residuals' if i == 0 else None)
plt.xlabel('X')
plt.ylabel('Y')
plt.xlim(0, 100)
plt.ylim(-150, 600)
plt.legend(loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```


---

## {#section-test-data data-menu-title="Test Data" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Test data and the test MSE</p>
</div>

<!-- ---
## {#exam-analogy data-menu-title="Exam analogy" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">If you were going to take a final exam, how would you prefer the instructor to create the questions?</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">1. Take questions from assignments you already completed this quarter.</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">2. Come up with new questions testing the material covered this quarter.</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">On which set of questions would you expect to do better?</p>
</div> -->

---

## {#test-data data-menu-title="Test data"}

[Test data]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

A model will generally perform better when evaluated on the data it was trained on. 

But in most cases, we want to use our model to make predictions on previously unseen data. 

<br>

. . .

**Test data** is data that is **not part of the training dataset**, used to evaluate how well our model generalizes to previously unseen observations.

<br>

. . .

When we have enough data, we divide our observations into two groups:


::: {.body-text-m}
- [**Training set:**]{.teal-text} used to fit the model and estimate $\hat{f}$

- [**Test set:**]{.teal-text} held out until after training, used only to evaluate performance
:::

---

## {#train-test-split data-menu-title="Train/test split"}

[Splitting data into train and test sets]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false
plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
plt.plot(x, true_f(x), 'k-', linewidth=2, label='f (true)')
plt.scatter(x_obs, y_obs, s=30, facecolors='none', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, label='Train data')
plt.scatter(x_test, y_test, s=30, facecolors='none', edgecolors='red', zorder=5, label='Test data')
plt.xlabel('X')
plt.ylabel('Y')
plt.xlim(0, 100)
plt.ylim(-150, 600)
plt.legend(loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#test-mse data-menu-title="Test MSE"}

[Test MSE]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

When we compute the MSE on the test data, we get the **test MSE**.

We generally care much more about the test MSE than the training MSE.

<br>

**Check-in:** Intuitively, what do you expect to be larger: the training MSE or the test MSE?

---

## {#test-mse-viz data-menu-title="Visualizing the test MSE"}

[Visualizing the test MSE]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Our linear model $\hat{f}(x)=$ `{python} eq_string` has the following residuals on the test set, with a test MSE of `{python} round(test_mse, 2)`.

```{python}
plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
plt.plot(x, y_linear, color='orange', linewidth=2, label=r'$\hat{f}$')
plt.scatter(x_test, y_test, s=30, facecolors='none', edgecolors='red', zorder=5, label='Test data')

# Residual lines from test observations to linear fit
for i, (xi, yi, yhi) in enumerate(zip(x_test, y_test, y_hat_test)):
    plt.plot([xi, xi], [yi, yhi], 'r--', linewidth=0.8,
             label='Residuals' if i == 0 else None)

plt.xlabel('X')
plt.ylabel('Y')
plt.xlim(0, 100)
plt.ylim(-150, 600)
plt.legend(loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#section-overfitting data-menu-title="# Overfitting #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Overfitting</p>
</div>

---

## {#overfitting-def data-menu-title="What is overfitting?"}

[Overfitting]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

We say a model is **overfitting** when it has a:

::: {.body-text-m}
- **small training MSE**, but a
- **large test MSE**
:::

and a simpler model would have achieved a lower test MSE.

<br>

. . .

::: {.body-text-m .gray-text}
> "Our learning procedure is working too hard to find patterns in the training data, and may be picking up some patterns that are just caused by random chance rather than by true properties of $f$." — *ISLP*
:::

---

## {#three-models data-menu-title="Three models fitted"}

[Three models, same training data]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false
plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
plt.scatter(x_obs, y_obs, s=30, facecolors='none', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, label='"Observed" data')
plt.plot(x, y_linear,  color='orange',    linewidth=2, label='Linear')
plt.plot(x, y_poly3,   color='steelblue', linewidth=2, label='Degree 3 polynomial')
plt.plot(x, y_poly15,  color='green',     linewidth=2, label='Degree 15 polynomial')
plt.xlabel('X')
plt.ylabel('Y')
plt.xlim(0, 100)
plt.ylim(-150, 600)
plt.legend(fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

::: {.center-text .body-text-s .teal-text}
Which model do you think will have the lowest training MSE? The lowest test MSE?
:::

---

## {#train-test-mse data-menu-title="Train vs. test MSE"}

[Training MSE vs. test MSE]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false
plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x * 0.6, fig_size_y))
plt.plot(flexibility, train_mses, '-', color='gray', linewidth=2, label='Training MSE')
plt.plot(flexibility, test_mses,  '-', color='red',  linewidth=2, label='Test MSE')
for i, (f, c) in enumerate(zip(flexibility, colors)):
    plt.plot(f, train_mses[i], 's', color=c, markersize=8)
    plt.plot(f, test_mses[i],  's', color=c, markersize=8)
plt.xlabel('Model flexibility (polynomial degree)')
plt.ylabel('Mean Squared Error')
plt.xticks(flexibility, ['1 (linear)', '3', '15'])
plt.legend(loc='upper center')
plt.grid(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

. . .

::: {.body-text-m}
As flexibility increases, training MSE [always]{.teal-text} decreases — but the test MSE may go back up!
:::

---

## {#checkin-2 data-menu-title="Check-in 2" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">Which model is overfitting, and why?</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">Does a better fit on the training data always indicate good performance on unseen data?</p>
</div>

---

## {#section-bias-variance data-menu-title="# Bias-Variance Tradeoff #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">The bias-variance tradeoff</p>
</div>

---

## {#model-variance data-menu-title="Model variance"}

[Model variance]{.slide-title}

<hr>

**Model variance** is how much $\hat{f}$ would change if we trained it on a different dataset drawn from the same population.

<br>

. . .

::: {.body-text-m}
- [**High-variance model:**]{.teal-text} very sensitive to the specific training data — a small change in the data produces a very different $\hat{f}$

- [**Low-variance model:**]{.teal-text} relatively stable across different training sets
:::

<br>

. . .

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
Which do you think has higher variance: a linear model or a degree-15 polynomial?
:::

---

## {#variance-demo data-menu-title="Variance visualization"}

[High vs. low variance]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Two training sets, same true $f$ — how much do the fitted models change?

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(fig_size_x * 0.6, fig_size_y))
ax.scatter(x_obs,   y_obs,   s=20, facecolors='none', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, label='Train 1')
ax.scatter(x_obs_2, y_obs_2, s=20, facecolors='none', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, marker='^', label='Train 2')
ax.plot(x, y_linear,   color='orange', linewidth=2, label='Linear fit 1')
ax.plot(x, y_linear_2, color='maroon', linewidth=2, label='Linear fit 2')
ax.set_xlabel('X')
ax.set_ylabel('Y')
ax.set_xlim(0, 100)
ax.set_ylim(-150, 600)
ax.set_title('Linear (low variance)')
ax.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize=7)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(fig_size_x * 0.6, fig_size_y))
ax.scatter(x_obs,   y_obs,   s=20, facecolors='none', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, label='Train 1')
ax.scatter(x_obs_2, y_obs_2, s=20, facecolors='none', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, marker='^', label='Train 2')
ax.plot(x, y_poly15,   color='forestgreen', linewidth=2, label='Degree 15 fit 1')
ax.plot(x, y_poly15_2, color='darkviolet',  linewidth=2, label='Degree 15 fit 2')
ax.set_xlabel('X')
ax.set_ylabel('Y')
ax.set_xlim(0, 100)
ax.set_ylim(-150, 600)
ax.set_title('Degree 15 (high variance)')
ax.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize=7)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```
:::

::::

---

## {#model-bias data-menu-title="Model bias"}

[Model bias]{.slide-title}

<hr>

**Model bias** is the error introduced by the assumptions built into the model.

<br>

. . .

::: {.body-text-m}
- [**High-bias model:**]{.teal-text} its assumptions are too restrictive to capture the true shape of $f$ — it will be systematically wrong no matter how much data we give it

- [**Low-bias model:**]{.teal-text} flexible enough to approximate the true $f$
:::

<br>

. . .

::: {.body-text-m .gray-text}
Example: if the true $f$ is cubic but we fit a linear model, the linear model simply cannot capture the true shape — it will always be biased.
:::

---

## {#bv-decomposition data-menu-title="Bias-variance decomposition"}

[The bias-variance decomposition]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

From a theoretical perspective, the **expected test MSE** decomposes as:

$$\text{Expected test MSE} = \underbrace{\text{Model variance}}_{\text{sensitivity to training data}} + \underbrace{\text{Model bias}^2}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Irreducible error}}_{\text{Var}(\epsilon)}$$

<br>

. . .

- The **irreducible error** $\text{Var}(\epsilon)$ is always present — it sets a lower bound on the test MSE

- **Model variance** and **bias** both contribute to error, but depend on the model we choose

. . .

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
To minimize the expected test MSE, we need *both* low variance and low bias.
:::

---

## {#the-tradeoff data-menu-title="The tradeoff"}

[The bias-variance tradeoff]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m .gray-text}
> "As we use more flexible methods, the variance will increase and the bias will decrease. The relative rate of change of these two quantities determines whether the test MSE increases or decreases." — *ISLP*
:::

. . .

```{python}
#| echo: false
plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x * 0.65, fig_size_y))
plt.plot(flexibility, train_mses, '-', color='gray', linewidth=2, label='Training MSE')
plt.plot(flexibility, test_mses,  '-', color='red',  linewidth=2, label='Test MSE')
for i, (f, c) in enumerate(zip(flexibility, colors)):
    plt.plot(f, train_mses[i], 's', color=c, markersize=8)
    plt.plot(f, test_mses[i],  's', color=c, markersize=8)
plt.xlabel('Model flexibility (polynomial degree)')
plt.ylabel('Mean Squared Error')
plt.xticks(flexibility, ['1 (linear)', '3', '15'])
plt.legend(loc='upper center')
plt.grid(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

::: {.body-text-s}
From degree 1 → 3: bias drops faster than variance rises → test MSE falls.

From degree 3 → 15: variance rises faster than bias drops → test MSE climbs back up.
:::

---

## {#checkin-3 data-menu-title="Check-in 3" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">Looking at the test MSE plot, which model would you select and why?</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">Would you consider evaluating polynomials with other degrees?</p>
</div>

---

## {#recap data-menu-title="Recap"}

[Recap]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

- The **training MSE** measures how well $\hat{f}$ fits the training data — but we care more about the **test MSE**

- A model that fits training data too closely may **overfit**: low training MSE, high test MSE

- **Model variance** measures how sensitive $\hat{f}$ is to the specific training set used

- **Model bias** measures systematic error from restrictive model assumptions

- Expected test MSE = variance + bias² + irreducible error

- The **bias-variance tradeoff**: more flexible models have lower bias but higher variance

<br>

::: {.center-text .teal-text}
The challenge is finding a model where both variance *and* bias are reasonably low.
:::
